metadata.description: KL散度: KL Divergence (Kullback-Leibler Divergence): \(D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \sum P(x) \log \left(\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\right)\) This measures the difference between two probability distributions, and it is not symmetric. Python code example: ```python import numpy as np def kl_divergence(p, q): return np.sum(np.where(p != 0, p * np.log(p / q), 0)) # Example usage p = np.array([0.2, 0.3, 0.5]) q = np.array([0.1, 0.4, 0.5]) print(kl_divergence(p, q)) ``` This Python function calculates the KL divergence between two probability distributions `p` and `q`. Note that the KL divergence is not symmetric, meaning \(D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \parallel P)\).

data_pointers.title: KL散度
data_pointers.content_preview: KL散度: KL Divergence (Kullback-Leibler Divergence): \(D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \sum P(x) \log \left(\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\right)\) This measures the difference between two probability distributions, and it is not symmetric. Python code example: ```python import numpy as np def kl_divergence(p, q): return np.sum(np.where(p != 0, p * np.log(p / q), 0)) # Example usage p = np.array([0.2, 0.3, 0.5]) q = np.array([0.1, 0.4, 0.5]) print(kl_divergence(p, q)) ``` This Python function calculates the KL divergence between two probability distributions `p` and `q`. Note that the KL divergence is not symmetric, meaning \(D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \parallel P)\).
